linkedin_data.headline: Engineering Manager @ Zepto | Ex-Flipkart | Building high-velocity teams
linkedin_data.summary: I've spent the last 8 years scaling engineering teams in India's fast-commerce space. Outside of work, I'm an avid ultramarathon runner — completed the Ladakh 50K last year. I read voraciously (mostly non-fiction on systems thinking and behavioural economics). Currently geeking out on productivity tooling and async workflows.
recent_posts: Just crossed the finish line at the Auroville Half-Marathon. 6 months of training, a lot of 5am runs, and an embarrassing number of energy gels.
Finished 'The Almanack of Naval Ravikant' last weekend. The chapter on specific knowledge reshaping the way I think about how I hire.
Hot take: async-first culture is the biggest productivity unlock for distributed engineering teams and almost nobody does it right.
recent_comments: Agree completely — the difference between a good and great engineer often isn't technical skill, it's the ability to communicate tradeoffs clearly.
Been using Obsidian for 18 months now. The bi-directional links are genuinely useful once your note graph gets large enough.
volunteer_work: Mentors first-gen college students at iMentor India

constraints.preferences_noted: Travels light, values quality over quantity

linkedin_data.headline: Senior Product Designer @ Swiggy | Design Systems | ex-Zomato
linkedin_data.summary: I design things that millions of people tap on while hungry. Passionate about accessibility, motion design, and making complex flows feel effortless. Off-screen I paint watercolours, practice Bharatanatyam, and grow herbs on my Mumbai balcony.
recent_posts: Shipped our new design system token library this week. 400 components, dark mode baked in from day one. The accessibility audit came back green for the first time ever.
Spent the weekend at a watercolour workshop in Bandra. There's something deeply meditative about wet-on-wet technique — you can plan all you want but the paint decides.
Hot take: most onboarding flows fail because designers solve for 'first time' but forget 'second time'. The aha moment needs to happen again on day 3.
recent_comments: This is exactly why I switched to variable fonts last year — the performance gains on mobile are massive.
Totally agree on the motion design point. Easing curves are the punctuation of interaction design.
volunteer_work: Teaches free design basics to underprivileged youth at Design for Change

linkedin_data.headline: VP Engineering @ Razorpay | FinTech Infrastructure | Angel Investor
linkedin_data.summary: Building the payments backbone for Indian internet. Led the team that scaled Razorpay from 10K to 5M merchants. I write about distributed systems, reliability engineering, and the craft of building high-stakes software. Weekend hobby: amateur astrophotography from my terrace in Bengaluru.
recent_posts: We just processed ₹1 lakh crore in a single quarter. Thread on the architectural decisions that made it possible without a single P0 incident 🧵
Captured Saturn's rings last night with my 8-inch Dobsonian. 3 hours of stacking 2000 frames. The patience required is not unlike debugging a race condition in prod.
Invested in my 12th startup this year. The one thing all the founding teams that impressed me had in common: they had deeply thought through their failure modes.
recent_comments: The CAP theorem is often misunderstood — you're not choosing two out of three, you're choosing your failure mode.
Great thread. I'd add that on-call culture is a leading indicator of engineering culture overall.

constraints.preferences_noted: Values experiences and precision instruments over decorative items
